coredata.dc:title: A panchromatic graphite-derived-carbon-dot TiO2 photocatalyst: Broad visible light absorption via ligand-to-metal charge transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Yoonsang
Bui, Hoang Tran
Sohn, Yurim
Park, Cheolwoo
Kim, Bupmo
Lee, Taehyung
Kim, Daegun
Lee, Dajin
Cho, Kilwon
Kim, Hyo Won
Kwon, Woosung
Kim, Wooyul
coredata.dc:description: In recent decades, solar-to-chemical conversion catalysts for energy and environmental applications have drawn significant attention. Catalysts composed of nano-size TiO2 and various dye-based photosensitizers can absorb solar light efficiently and produce promising photocatalysts, but their photoactive region is limited. In this study, we demonstrate that nanocomposites composed of graphite-derived carbon dots (GCDs) and TiO2 are capable of ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), which was active across the entire visible-light spectrum. The effect of GCDs on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 was determined through the production of hydrogen peroxide and the degradation of pollutants. On the basis of chemical and optical investigation, we demonstrate the formation of an LMCT band in the TiO2/GCD nanocomposites, which are responsible for photoactivation below the bandgap of each component. Our research provides conclusive evidence for the presence of the LMCT band in TiO2/GCD and reveals a novel method for expanding the light-absorption spectrum of the photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon dots
Titania
Panchromatic absorption

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Special section on Materials Challenges in Alternative and Renewable Energy 2022 (MCARE 2022)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoinduced charge transfer in TiO2/GCD via LMCT
Photocatalytic production of H2O2 with TiO2/GCD

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A5A1084921, NRF-2022R1A2C4002403, and NRF-2022M3A9F3017700 (the Bio & Medical Technology Development Program)) and the Human Resources Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Korea government Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (No. 20224000000320).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic of the synthesis of the TiO2/GCD nanocomposites. (b) TEM images of TiO2/GCDs. SAED patterns of TiO2 and the carbon lattice (yellow and red-dotted lines, respectively). The lattice spacings of TiO2 and carbon are labelled as yellow and red, respectively. The scale bar is 2 nm. (c) FT-IR spectra of TiO2, TiO2/GCD, and GCD. (d) XPS C1s spectra of TiO2/GCD. (e) Zeta-potential of TiO2 and TiO2/GCD in relation to pH. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Time-resolved PL spectra of GCD and TiO2/GCDs. (b) DR UV–vis spectra of TiO2 (1) and TiO2/GCD (2). The colored area shows LMCT absorption. (c) Difference spectrum (i.e., 2–1), UV–vis absorption spectrum of GCD, and wavelength-dependent activity of TiO2/GCD for H2O2 generation. Experimental conditions for H2O2 generation: [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L, pH = 3.0, stirred in dark for 30 min with O2 purging (400 mL/min), followed by irradiation (300-W Xe lamp, λ ≥ 420 nm). (d) UPS spectrum of GCD (inset: magnified spectrum at HOMO region). (e) Proposed energy levels of TiO2/GCD (left) and schematic of the LMCT transition (right).

(a) H2O2 generation of TiO2 and TiO2/GCD under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm and λ ≥ 610 nm). (b) H2O2 generation of TiO2 and TiO2/GCD as a function of irradiation wavelength. (c) Decomposition rate of H2O2 under visible-light irradiation. Experimental conditions: [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L, pH = 3.0, stirred in dark for 30 min with O2 purging (400 mL/min), followed by irradiation (300-W Xe lamp, λ ≥ 420 nm) (for (a) and (c)) and 5% isopropanol (for (a) and (b)), [H2O2]0 = 4 mM (for (c)). (d) Recycling tests of TiO2/GCD for H2O2 generation under visible-light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm and λ ≥ 610 nm). Experimental conditions: [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L, 5% isopropanol, pH = 3.0, and O2 purging (400 mL/min).

(a) 4-CP reduction and (b) Cr6+ photodegradation of TiO2 and TiO2/GCD under visible illumination (λ ≥ 420 nm-solid symbol and λ ≥ 610 nm-open symbol); the control data were collected in the dark (solid triangle) and in the absence of catalyst (opened triangle). (c) Difference DRS spectrum of TiO2/GCD (2) and TiO2 (1) (i.e., 2 – 1) (spectra (1) and (2) are shown in Fig. 2(b)) and the wavelength-dependent activity of TiO2/GCD for 4-CP reduction. Experimental conditions: [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L, [4-CP]0 = 100 µM, pH 3.0. (d) Difference spectrum of TiO2/GCD (2) and TiO2 (1) (i.e., 2–1) and the wavelength-dependent activity of TiO2/GCD for Cr6+ photodegradation. Experimental conditions: [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L, [Cr6+]0 = 200 µM, pH 3.0.
PL lifetimes of GCD and TiO2/GCDs.

head.ce:title: A panchromatic graphite-derived-carbon-dot TiO2 photocatalyst: Broad visible light absorption via ligand-to-metal charge transfer

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent decades, solar-to-chemical conversion catalysts for energy and environmental applications have drawn significant attention. Catalysts composed of nano-size TiO2 and various dye-based photosensitizers can absorb solar light efficiently and produce promising photocatalysts, but their photoactive region is limited. In this study, we demonstrate that nanocomposites composed of graphite-derived carbon dots (GCDs) and TiO2 are capable of ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), which was active across the entire visible-light spectrum. The effect of GCDs on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 was determined through the production of hydrogen peroxide and the degradation of pollutants. On the basis of chemical and optical investigation, we demonstrate the formation of an LMCT band in the TiO2/GCD nanocomposites, which are responsible for photoactivation below the bandgap of each component. Our research provides conclusive evidence for the presence of the LMCT band in TiO2/GCD and reveals a novel method for expanding the light-absorption spectrum of the photocatalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 is widely employed in photocatalysis research due to its favorable physical features, chemical stability, eco-friendliness, and numerous applications (e.g., in the energy industry and for environmental remediation) [1–5]. However, the wide bandgap of TiO2 (about 3.2 eV) prohibits the visible light absorption (400–800 nm) [6,7]. In order to accomplish visible-light-driven TiO2 catalysis, numerous strategies have been implemented, including bandgap engineering via noble metal doping, heterostructure formation via conjunction with other metal oxides, and dye-sensitization [8–16]. Of these approaches, the photosensitization of TiO2, especially dye sensitization, is extensively employed and is a simple and highly efficient method for forming an electron-transfer pathway and extending the photoresponse of TiO2 to the visible range [17]. In this method, electrons are transferred from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the dye to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 [18–21]. In contrast to other modification techniques such as noble metal doping or heterostructure formation, dye sensitizers (i.e., chromophores) may absorb light over a broad range of wavelengths due to the large variety of available dyes and their adaptable light absorption. Moreover, many of these dyes can be prepared using simple and gentle synthetic methods [22–24]. However, successful sensitizers are primarily restricted to the noble metal-based complexes (e.g. Ru-complex, Pt-complex, etc.), despite the development of low cost alternatives. In addition, the efficient visible light sensitization requires chemical anchoring groups (such as carboxylate, phosphonate, and catechol linkage) for a strong chemical bond between the semiconductor surface and the sensitizer molecule [21]. However, this anchoring chemical bond can only be formed within a narrow pH range and is not naturally stable in aquatic environments [25,26].

As an alternative method for photosensitization, ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) includes the development of a direct electron transfer pathway from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the adsorbate to the CB of the photocatalyst [18]. Importantly, LMCT complexes are formed with TiO2 via functional group interactions (Table S1). Unlike dye sensitizers, which are easily degraded when exposed to light, LMCT sensitizers can be firmly anchored on the TiO2 surface, allowing for cyclical usage of the resulting catalyst. As an LMCT sensitizer, carbon-based nanomaterials have drawn a significant deal of interest due to their chemical tunability of anchoring groups, sustainability, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness. However, the majority of reported carbon-coated TiO2 photocatalysts (TiO2@C) only exhibit photocatalytic activity in a narrow region of the visible spectrum (400–500 nm or 700–800 nm) [27–29], limiting their ability to fully harness their catalytic properties.

In this study, we synthesized graphite-derived carbon dots (GCDs), a carbon-based semiconductor nanoparticle, as an LMCT photosensitizer to construct a panchromatic photocatalytic system that is active over the whole visible-light spectrum. GCDs were chosen as a TiO2 photosensitizer since it has been reported that they possess an adjustable bandgap, excellent water solubility, and chemical stability. In contrast to other carbon nanoparticles with a quasi-spherical amorphous carbon core and oxidized surface functional groups, GCDs have a graphene oxide structure composed of several graphene layers, which confers advantages for electron transport and light absorption [30–33]. Furthermore, we synthesized TiO2/GCD nanocomposites that take advantage of the strong interactions between TiO2 and GCDs, which led to the development of the LMCT band. Consequently, the produced nanocomposites exhibited enhanced H2O2 generation and pollutant degradation upon illumination over the whole visible-light spectrum (400–800 nm) as well as excellent stability after repeated use in aqueous environments. The kinetics of LMCT at the TiO2/GCD interface and the effects of GCDs on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 have been thoroughly investigated using photocatalytic techniques.

P25 titanium dioxide nanopowder (rutile:anatase ratio of 80:20; 99.9%, primary particle size of 20–30 nm) was purchased from Degussa and used as the base catalyst material. Graphite powder (<20 µm) was obtained from Aldrich. The following chemicals were used as received: N,N-diethyl-1,4-phenylenediamine sulfate (DPD, Aldrich, 97%), horseradish peroxidase (POD, Aldrich), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, Aldrich, 95–98%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, Aldrich, ≥ 85 wt% in H2O), sodium dichromate dihydrate (Na2Cr2O7·2H2O, Aldrich, ≥ 99.5 %), 1,5-diphenylcarbazide (DPC, Aldrich), methanol (MeOH) for high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Aldrich, ≥ 99.9%), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP, Aldrich), hydrogen peroxide for photochemical experiments (H2O2, Junsei and Aldrich, 30% in H2O), acetonitrile (ACN, J. T. Baker, HPLC ultra-gradient solvent), isopropanol (Aldrich, ≥ 99.5%), potassium perchlorate (KClO4, Aldrich, ≥ 99%), nafion perfluorinated resin solution (Aldrich). Other reagents used for experiments in this work were analytical grade and used as received.

GCDs were synthesized following a standard method for the chemical oxidation of graphite with some modifications [34]. Briefly, graphite powder (100 mg) was added to a 3:1 (v/v) mixture of sulfuric and nitric acids and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then refluxed for 24 h at 100 °C. After the solution had cooled to room temperature, water (100 mL) was added, and the solution was filtered to remove the solid residue. The filtrate was dialyzed against water for 7 days (24 h for each cycle) using Spectra/Por Biotech cellulose ester dialysis tubes (100–500 Da). The water was removed from the resulting solution by freeze-drying, and the resulting solid was stored in a freezer for further use.

First, 500 mg of TiO2 powder was dispersed in 15 mL of ethanol/water (1:2 v/v) with vigorous stirring. To the suspension, 1, 4, or 6 mg/mL solutions of GCD (0.5 mL) were added with vigorous stirring to yield TiO2/GCD 0.1, 0.4, and 0.6, respectively, which are the weight percentages of GCD relative to TiO2. After stirring for 2 days, 50 mL of water was added, and the photocatalyst was precipitated by centrifugation (4000 rpm for 10 min) and redispersed in water. This process was repeated three times to remove any residuals. The final product of TiO2/GCD was dried in a vacuum oven (80 °C) overnight and then stored in a powder form.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Jeol JEM-2200FS equipped with a Cs corrector. X-ray powder diffractometry (XRD) experiments were performed using a Bruker New D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy was performed using a Horiba LabRAM HV Evolution spectrometer at a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer using an attenuated total reflection (ATR) accessory. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Escalab 250 spectrometer with an Al X-ray source (1486.6 eV). UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) speoctroscopy measurements were performed using a FluoTime-200 system (PicoQuant) with a variable repetition rate (0.8 MHz). Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) was performed with He I source (21.2 eV) at the 4B1 beamline of the Pohang Accelerator Laboratory in Korea.

The photocatalytic generation of hydrogen peroxide was achieved using a 300-W Xe arc lamp (Newport) with a cutoff filter (320 nm and 420 and 610 nm for UV and visible light, respectively). The pH of the suspension was controlled by the addition of HClO4 and/or NaOH. The H2O2 production experiments were carried out under ambient conditions in an open reactor. O2 gas was purged into the reactor for 30 min before irradiation to ensure the formation of O2-saturated media. Subsequently, O2 purging was continuously carried out during the reaction. Every 30 min, a 6-mL sample was collected using a syringe and filtered through a 0.45-µm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter (Whatman), and the H2O2 contents were determined using the DPD method [35]. The concentration of Cr6+ was determined using the DPC colorimetric method [36]. The amount of 4-CP present was monitored using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC: LC-20 CE pump, Shimadzu) with a diode array detector (DAD) and a Shim-pack GIS column (4.6 mm × 250 mm). A mixture of 0.1 % H3PO4 solution and acetonitrile was used as the eluent.

Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were performed on the Pt ring/glass carbon disk electrode using rotating ring disk electrode apparatus (RRDE-3A). The TiO2/GCD sample (1.0 mg) was dispersed in 600 μL isopropanol, and 6 μL nafion solution. After sonicating for 30 min, 15 μL of the homogeneous suspension was dropped on the electrode surface, and let it dry slowly in open air. The RDE loaded with TiO2/GCD sample, an Ag/AgCl electrode, and Pt wire were used as the working, reference, and counter electrode, respectively.

Fig. 1 (a) depicts a schematic of the synthetic process and the nanocomposite structure. The GCDs utilized in this study were produced by oxidizing graphite in a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 [34]. The GCDs and TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were mixed and stirred in an ethanol/water solution to form GCD-decorated TiO2 (TiO2/GCD) nanocomposites. In Fig. 1(b), the TEM images reveal that the GCDs with a particle size of approximately 5 nm were attached to the surface of TiO2 NPs approximately 25 nm in diameter. The high-resolution TEM and selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns reveal the distinct lattice structures of TiO2 and graphitic carbon with the lattice spacings of 0.35 and 0.21 nm attributed to the (101) anatase TiO2 plane and the (100) graphene plane, respectively (Fig. 1(b), inset) [37].

Using FTIR spectroscopy, the interactions between the GCDs and TiO2 NPs were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 1(c), the FTIR spectrum of the TiO2/GCD composites reveals that the 1730 cm−1 band corresponding to carboxyl (CO) groups has disappeared, while the 1600 cm−1 band corresponding to asymmetric COO- stretching has appeared, indicating that the GCDs are electrostatically coupled with TiO2 via the carboxylate anions (COO–) and surface titanol group (≡Ti − OH2 +) [38]. The XPS spectra and elemental analysis indicate that the GCDs were successfully combined with TiO2 (Fig. S1 and Table S2). In addition, the XPS C1s spectra reveal the presence of C C/CC (284.8 eV), CO (286 eV), CO (287.6 eV), and OC = O (289 eV) bonds, which result from the highly oxidized carbon structure of the GCDs (Fig. 1(d)) [34]. The GCDs have extremely negative zeta potentials [39,40] due to the plethora of oxygen-containing surface functional groups. Consequently, the TiO2/GCD composites had significantly more negative zeta potentials than TiO2 across all pH regions (Fig. 1(e)). As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD patterns and Raman spectra of the TiO2/GCD composites exhibit only the characteristic peaks of TiO2 and no characteristic peaks from GCDs; this is likely due to the low crystallinity of the GCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoinduced charge transfer in TiO2/GCD via LMCT
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the mechanism of photoinduced charge transfer in the TiO2/GCD composites, TRPL spectroscopy and diffuse reflectance (DR) UV–visible absorption spectroscopy were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2
(a) and listed in Table 1 , the TRPL measurements reveal that the addition of the GCDs accelerated PL decay. Specifically, the average charge-carrier lifetime of TiO2/GCD (0.765 ns) is shorter than that of GCD (0.987 ns), indicating that there is an efficient electron transfer pathway from the HOMO of the GCDs to the CB of TiO2. In Fig. 2(b), the DR UV–visible absorption spectra clearly demonstrate the increased light absorption of TiO2/GCD throughout the entire visible-light spectrum, whereas TiO2 exhibits no absorption in the same range. Intriguingly, the light absorption by TiO2/GCD is more pronounced than that of GCD alone and extends even further into the lower energy region (Fig. 2(c)). This suggests that the formation of the TiO2/GCD nanocomposites results in an additional electronic transition.

To order to identify the origin and photocatalytic activity of the extra electronic transition, the dependence of the rate of H2O2 generation on the wavelength of incident light was investigated (solid red line in Fig. 2(c)). Except for a slight dip at 450 nm, which can be attributed to the absorption of light by H2O2 adsorbed on the surface of TiO2 [41], the overall trend of the generation rates corresponds more closely to the LMCT absorption tail of TiO2/GCD rather than that of GCD alone. In addition, the generated current density (j) of TiO2/GCD increased with increasing photon energy (i.e., visible wavelength), indicating that the GCDs deposited on the TiO2 surface contributed to the generation of electron-hole pairs in TiO2/GCD system (Fig. S3). This suggests that the new electronic transition is not accountable for the typical photosensitization (i.e., GCD as chromophore in TiO2/GCD).

In order to determine the origin of the new electronic transition, the electronic states of the GCDs were examined by UPS and steady-state PL spectroscopy. According to Fig. 2(d), the HOMO of the GCDs is located at − 5.65 eV, and the optical bandgap of the GCDs was calculated to be 2.61 eV, which is consistent with the excitation peak (475 nm) observed in the PL excitation spectra of the GCDs (Fig. S4). Combining this information with previously reported literature on the electronic states of TiO2 [42], we propose an energy level diagram for TiO2/GCD in Fig. 4 (e). Considering the well-aligned energy levels of TiO2/GCD in this model, it is plausible that an LMCT takes place between the HOMO of GCD to the CB of TiO2. Because electron-rich adsorbates with functional groups such as carboxyl groups can strengthen electronic coupling and induce the appearance of LMCT bands [18], the presence of carboxylic acid interactions between GCD and TiO2 may also facilitate LMCT complex formation. According to the energy level model, the calculated energy of the LMCT transition is approximately 1.7–1.95 eV, or 635–730 nm, which corresponds well to the extended light-absorption region of TiO2/GCD. As a result of LMCT, the TiO2/GCD composites can absorb light at longer wavelengths of visible light, enabling photocatalytic production of H2O2 across the entire visible-light region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic production of H2O2 with TiO2/GCD
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the role of the GCD layer on H2O2 production, the photocatalytic performance of pure TiO2 NPs and TiO2/GCD composites was compared (Fig. 3, S5, and S6(a)). The formation of H2O2 on TiO2/GCD was significantly enhanced in comparison to TiO2. This can be explained as follows: under UV and visible-light irradiation, the ground-state electron of GCD was directly excited and transferred to the CB of TiO2. The electron then participates in the photocatalytic production of H2O2 via the two-electron transfer reaction with molecular oxygen. The photoactivity of TiO2/GCD was further evaluated by monitoring H2O2 production under varying visible light wavelengths (Fig. 3(a) and 3(b)). Specifically, the photocatalytic activity for H2O2 production at wavelengths greater than 500 nm demonstrates an extension of visible light absorption via LMCT. In addition, the generation of H2O2 was negligible when the GCDs and TiO2 NPs were separately exposed (as control tests) to visible to infrared light (Fig. 3(a) and 3(b)). This result demonstrates that GCD cannot reduce O2 on their own; however, the addition of GCD to the TiO2 surface enhanced the production of H2O2 over nearly the entire visible-light spectrum.

Overall, H2O2 photogeneration involves both its production and degradation simultaneously [6]. In other words, the overall photocatalytic efficiency is enhanced by both enhancing O2 photoreduction and preventing H2O2 decomposition. Comparing the formation (kf ) and decomposition (k d) rate constants of TiO2 and TiO2/GCD (Fig. S5) reveals that the presence of GCD had a significant effect on the H2O2 decomposition rate compared to that of TiO2 alone; specifically, there was a significant increase in overall H2O2 photogeneration on TiO2/GCD. Furthermore, the photocatalytic decomposition of H2O2 on TiO2/GCD was significantly diminished in the absence of a hole scavenger (i.e., isopropanol) (Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S6(b)). Under visible-light irradiation, the formation of surface titanium(IV)–hydrogen peroxide complexes (⋮TiIV–OOH) causes the degradation of H2O2 over pure TiO2 [41]. Therefore, the suppression of H2O2 decomposition on TiO2/GCD is due to the inhibition of the interaction between the TiO2 surface and H2O2 molecules. Importantly, after repeated cycles of H2O2 production under visible-light irradiation (wavelengths greater than 420 and 610 nm, respectively), TiO2/GCD remained stable (Fig. 3(d)). As shown in Fig. S7, the optical property and chemical bonding (carboxylic linkage) of TiO2/GCDs were preserved after photocatalytic test. Thus, carbon nanostructure (i.e., GCD) as adsorbates not only extended the visible light absorption region but also enhanced the photostability of the LMCT photocatalytic system. Under UV irradiation, however, the carboxylic-acid-based linkage between TiO2 and GCD was weakened (Fig. S6(c)), and after two cycles of H2O2 production, GCD was damaged and began peeling off the surface of TiO2, resulting in a decrease in the amount of H2O2 produced. This indicates that the formation of LMCT interactions in the TiO2/GCD composites is crucial for the visible-light-driven activity of TiO2. The improvement in H2O2 production over TiO2 is proportional to the amount of GCD added (Fig. S8). Specifically, the production of H2O2 initially increased as the amount of added GCD increased; however, when the GCD amount was too high (above 0.4 wt%), the active sites were blocked by the excess GCD, resulting in a decrease in H2O2 production.

To investigate further the effect of LMCT on the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/GCD, we conducted experiments on pollutant degradation under UV and visible light irradiation (Fig. 4 and S9). Cr6+ complexes and 4-CP were selected as the pollutants of interest. The photocatalytic degradation (PCD) of the two substrates occurs in almost the entire visible-light spectrum (Fig. 4(a) and (b)), which is consistent with the visible light-induced H2O2 production (Fig. 3). In contrast, upon UV irradiation, the PCD rates for TiO2/GCD did not change or were even slower (Fig. S9). These results indicate that the LMCT effect is negligible under UV irradiation but significant under visible-light irradiation, even in the low-photon-energy region (Fig. 4(c) and (d)). Under UV-illuminated conditions, the activation (bandgap excitation) of TiO2 itself overpowered the LMCT activation of TiO2/GCD. In addition, the enhancement in the photogeneration of H2O2 over TiO2/GCD under UV irradiation (Fig. S6) may be primarily attributable to the inhibition of H2O2 decomposition on the TiO2/GCD composites rather than the extension of visible light absorption via LMCT.

Table S1 compares the photoactivity of the as-prepared TiO2/GCD composites to that of other LMCT systems in the literature. To date, studies of the LMCT effect have focused on TiO2 and substrate (i.e., surface adsorbates) complexes. However, these complexes are not stable because electron transfer rapidly degrades them. In contrast, the TiO2/GCD composites synthesized in this study is durable and can be used repeatedly under visible-light irradiation for multiple cycles. Intriguingly, their active region extends up to 800 nm in wavelength, which has not previously been reported for an LMCT mediated photocatalytic system.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated the extraordinary panchromatic photocatalytic activity of the TiO2/GCD nanocomposites. First, compared to organic adsorbates, GCD significantly enhanced the photostability of the LMCT photocatalytic system. We determined the energy levels of TiO2/GCD through optical analysis and concluded that the extended light absorption in the lower energy region is due to LMCT transitions between the HOMO of GCD and the CB of TiO2. According to our findings, TiO2/GCD can achieve photoresponse below the optical bandgap of chromophores by utilizing LMCT-mediated activation. These findings shed light on strategies for utilize the full visible-light. Consequently, we believe that this research will contribute to the development of a novel TiO2-based photocatalyst that is not only active in the visible spectrum, but may also be active in the near-infrared spectrum.

Yoonsang Park: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hoang Tran Bui: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yurim Sohn: Investigation. Cheolwoo Park: Investigation. Bupmo Kim: Investigation. Taehyung Lee: Investigation. Daegun Kim: Investigation. Dajin Lee: Investigation. Kilwon Cho: Investigation. Hyo Won Kim: Investigation. Woosung Kwon: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wooyul Kim: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. NRF-2021R1A5A1084921, NRF-2022R1A2C4002403, and NRF-2022M3A9F3017700 (the Bio & Medical Technology Development Program)) and the Human Resources Development of the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Korea government Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (No. 20224000000320).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155636.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
